title: Listening - Part 3: Spaces that Shape Us
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with an architect, Elliot, and a neuroscientist, Libby, about how the design of buildings affects our mental health. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview regarding the 'stress' of brutalist architecture, the benefits of 'biophilic' design, and why high ceilings lead to more creative thinking.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: We spend ninety percent of our time indoors. But how much do those four walls affect our brain? Today we’re exploring 'neuroarchitecture.' Libby, how does our brain react to different shapes?

Libby: [15] It’s fascinating. Sharp angles and hard edges actually trigger the amygdala—the part of the brain associated with fear and vigilance. [/15] In contrast, curved lines and rounded corners make us feel safe. Evolutionarily, sharp objects were often dangerous, while curves resemble the softness of nature. Our modern, boxy cities might be keeping us in a constant state of low-level stress.

Interviewer: Elliot, as an architect, are you moving away from 'the box'?

Elliot: [16] Definitely. We are embracing 'biophilic design'—the idea that humans have an innate need to connect with nature. [/16] It’s not just about adding a few plants. It’s about maximizing natural light, using organic materials like wood, and even mimicking the 'fractal' patterns you find in trees. Studies show that patients in hospitals with a view of greenery recover significantly faster than those facing a brick wall.

Interviewer: Libby, I’ve heard that the height of a ceiling can change how we think. Is that true?

Libby: [17] It’s called the 'Cathedral Effect.' High ceilings encourage abstract, creative thinking and a sense of freedom. [/17] Lower ceilings, on the other hand, are better for focused, detail-oriented work. This is why a studio should have a different design from a laboratory. We aren't just in the room; the room is in our mind.

Interviewer: Elliot, what about 'brutalist' architecture? Those huge concrete blocks are very controversial.

Elliot: [18] For many, they feel oppressive and cold. Concrete doesn't 'age' like stone or wood; it just decays. [/18] However, some argue that the simplicity is honest. But from a neuroarchitectural perspective, these buildings often fail because they provide no visual 'richness.' The human eye needs a certain level of complexity to stay engaged and calm.

Interviewer: Libby, how does noise and acoustics fit into this?

Libby: [19] Sound is often the 'forgotten' dimension of architecture. An open-plan office with high levels of 'reverberation'—echo—is a nightmare for productivity. [/19] It increases cortisol levels. We need 'acoustic privacy' as much as we need visual privacy. A well-designed building should sound as good as it looks.

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Elliot? Is this the future of city planning?

Elliot: [20] It has to be. We need to stop designing for 'efficiency' and start designing for 'humanity.' A building that is cheap to build but makes everyone inside it miserable is ultimately a failure. [/20]

Interviewer: Libby, Elliot, thank you.

questions.question: According to Libby, why do sharp angles in buildings cause stress?
options: A) They make it difficult for light to enter the room.
B) They trigger a brain response linked to danger.
C) They remind people of modern art which they dislike.
D) They are much harder for the eyes to focus on.
questions.explanation: She explains that sharp edges trigger the amygdala, associated with fear and vigilance.

questions.question: What is a core principle of 'biophilic design'?
options: A) Building as many square houses as possible.
B) Using only artificial light to save on heating costs.
C) Satisfying the human need to be connected to nature.
D) Designing hospitals that have no windows at all.
questions.explanation: Elliot defines it as the 'innate need to connect with nature', using plants and natural materials.

questions.question: The 'Cathedral Effect' suggests that high ceilings are best for
options: A) tasks that require a lot of focus and attention to detail.
B) scientific experiments that use sensitive equipment.
C) creative activities and thinking about abstract ideas.
D) sleeping in rooms that are very cold and dark.
questions.explanation: Libby says they encourage 'abstract, creative thinking and a sense of freedom'.

questions.question: In Elliot's opinion, why do many people dislike brutalist buildings?
options: A) They are too small for large families to live in.
B) They lack the visual complexity that humans need.
C) They use colors that are too bright for most streets.
D) They are too expensive to repair when they are old.
questions.explanation: He argues that they fail because they provide no 'visual richness' or complexity.

questions.question: Libby believes that open-plan offices can be bad for work because
options: A) they do not have enough desks for all the employees.
B) the high levels of echo increase stress and lower productivity.
C) the coworkers spend too much time talking to each other.
D) there is too much natural light coming from the windows.
questions.explanation: She mentions that high 'reverberation' (echo) increases cortisol and is a 'nightmare for productivity'.

questions.question: Elliot's final message is that architecture should prioritize
options: A) the speed and low cost of construction.
B) the happiness and well-being of the inhabitants.
C) the amount of concrete used in the foundation.
D) the height of the building in relation to other houses.
questions.explanation: He concludes that we should design for 'humanity' rather than just 'efficiency'.
